coredata.dc:title: Quasi-noble-metal graphene quantum dots deposited stannic oxide with oxygen vacancies: Synthesis and enhanced photocatalytic properties
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Quan, Bin
Liu, Wei
Liu, Yousong
Zheng, Ying
Yang, Guangcheng
Ji, Guangbin
coredata.dc:description: Quasi-noble-metal graphene quantum dots (GQDs) deposited stannic oxide (SnO2) with oxygen vacancies (VOs) were prepared by simply sintering SnO2 and citric acid (CA) together. The redox process between SnO2 and GQDs shows the formation of oxygen vacancy states below the conduction band of stannic oxide. The produced VOs obviously extend the optical absorption region of SnO2 to the visible-light region. Meanwhile, GQDs can effectively improve the charge-separation efficiency via a quasi function like noble metal and promote the visible-light response to some degree. In addition, the samples calcinated at 450°C reveals the best performance because of its relatively high concentrations of VOs. What is more, the possible degradation mechanism has been inferred as extended visible-light response as well as raised charge-separation efficiency has also been put forward. Our work may offer a simple strategy to combine the defect modulation and noble metal deposition simultaneously for efficient photocatalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalytic properties
Visible-light response
Oxygen vacancies
Graphene quantum dots
SnO2

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of the prepared samples (a) pure SnO2, (b) GQDs@SnO2 and (c–f) GQDs@SnO2-VOs-3, GQDs@SnO2-VOs-4, GQDs@SnO2-VOs-5, GQDs@SnO2-VOs-6.
Raman spectra of the prepared GQDs@SnO2 composites calcined at 350°C, 450°C, 550°C and 650°C, respectively.

TEM images of pure SnO2 samples (a–c); TEM (d–f) and HRTEM images (g and h) of GQDs@SnO2-VOs-4 samples. The insets in (g and h) show the corresponding FFT plots. (i) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of GQDs@SnO2-VOs-4 samples. Inset: the BJH pore-size distribution of the nanocomposite calculated from the desorption branch of N2 isotherm.
UV–vis absorbance spectra of the prepare samples.
EPR spectra of the prepared (a) SnO2 nanorods (b) GQDs/SnO2 composites and (c and d) GQDs@SnO2-VOs-4, GQDs@SnO2-VOs-5 nano-compounds. Inset photograph shows the color of the various samples.
TG curve of the samples GQDs@SnO2-VOs-3 to GQDs@SnO2-VOs-6.
Photodegradation of MB aqueous solutions and corresponding kinetic study under UV–vis light (a and b); visible light (c and d) irradiation over all the prepared samples.
Comparison of the degradation efficiency of GQDs@SnO2-VOs-4 composites for the photodegradation of MB on different scavengers.
Schematic diagram of the proposed mechanism for enhanced photocatalytic activity of GQDs@ SnO2-VOs nanocomposites under Xe arc lamp irradiation.

head.ce:title: Quasi-noble-metal graphene quantum dots deposited stannic oxide with oxygen vacancies: Synthesis and enhanced photocatalytic properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Technology, Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics, Nanjing 211100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Technology
Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemical Materials, China Academy of Engineering Physics, Mianyang, Sichuan 621900, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The nanocomposite photocatalysts of quasi-noble-metal graphene quantum dots (GQDs) deposited SnO2 with oxygen vacancies (VOs) were prepared through a simple two-step reactions process. The obtained nanocomposites exhibited much enhanced photodegradation activity for the extended visible-light response as well as raised charge-separation efficiency.

Quasi-noble-metal graphene quantum dots (GQDs) deposited stannic oxide (SnO2) with oxygen vacancies (VOs) were prepared by simply sintering SnO2 and citric acid (CA) together. The redox process between SnO2 and GQDs shows the formation of oxygen vacancy states below the conduction band of stannic oxide. The produced VOs obviously extend the optical absorption region of SnO2 to the visible-light region. Meanwhile, GQDs can effectively improve the charge-separation efficiency via a quasi function like noble metal and promote the visible-light response to some degree. In addition, the samples calcinated at 450°C reveals the best performance because of its relatively high concentrations of VOs. What is more, the possible degradation mechanism has been inferred as extended visible-light response as well as raised charge-separation efficiency has also been put forward. Our work may offer a simple strategy to combine the defect modulation and noble metal deposition simultaneously for efficient photocatalysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor photocatalysis has been widely regarded as a kind of potential technique which considerably meets the requirements for the current issues on environment and energy [1–3]. Earlier work done by Markham and co-workers described the phenomena of catalytic oxidation of organic compounds under UV light by some metal oxides like ZnO, Sb2O3, and TiO2. Decades of research have been carried on to answer many of the fundamental questions in regard to the intricacies of semiconductor photocatalysis [4–8]. However, the intrinsic issues of semiconductor photocatalysis still exist, and an ideal photocatalyst should own both a wide photo-absorption range and a low recombination rate of the photogenerated charge carriers [9–11].

Generally speaking, there are plenty of strategies to extend the scope of light response and increase the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs such as doping elements, defect engineering, noble metal deposition, coupling semiconductors with narrow-band semiconductors to form hybrid heterojunctions or composite [12–14]. Among these approaches, defect modulation has been regarded as an effective method to elongate photoresponse range which can be achieved by several synthetic approaches including doping with other elements, thermal treatment under oxygen depleted conditions, high energy bombardment and self-doping by reduction treatment [15,16]. Recently, self-doped semiconductor photocatalysts has attracted much attention. Ti3+-doped TiO2 has been reported to show visible-light absorption and enhanced photocatalytic activity. It has been verified that the Ti3+ species induced Ov states between the valance and the conduction bands is responsible for the ideal separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. In addition, the corresponding metal ion defects also extend the optical absorption of wide-bandgap semiconductors into the visible region [17–20]. Moreover, to reduce the recombination rate of the photogenerated charge carriers, depositing of noble metal is commonly used. Lately, GQDs has received much attention. Compared with two-dimensional graphene nanosheets and one dimensional nanoribbons, zero-dimensional GQDs possess strong quantum confinement and edge effects when their sizes are down to 10nm, which induce new physical properties. Hence, GQDs can be applied in many fields, such as photovoltaics, light-emitting diodes, and photocatalysis [21–23]. What is more, GQDs is quite cheap compared with noble metal like Pt, Pd, Ag which can affect the nature of the surface of semiconductor via altering the distribution of electron. Schottky barrier formed in the interface of GQDs and semiconductor captures excites electron and prevents the recombination of electron-hole pairs, thus improving the photocatalytic activities. Though there are numerous reports on defect modulation or depositing of noble metal [24,25], the reports integrating both two catalytic mechanisms of oxygen vacancies and GQDs-replacement noble metal together are rarely.

In this study, SnO2 was selected which has been widely used in photocatalysis, sensors, detectors, and solar cells [26,27] due to its low cost, nontoxicity, commercial availability and excellent chemical stability. Nevertheless, SnO2 is a poor absorber of photons in the visible portion of the solar spectrum limited by the wide energy band gap (3.6eV at 300K). Hence we employed a simple process to realize the integration of SnO2 and GQDs and the reduction of SnO2 by GQDs, which are produced by pyrolyzing of citric acid (CA). Oxygen vacancies (VOs) are generated after the redox reaction, meanwhile GQDs remain in the GQDs@SnO2 composite, taking part in the photocatalysis process. Thus we successfully accomplish a combination of two types to enhance photocatalytic performance in one semiconductor. Obviously, the GQDs@SnO2-VOs nanocomposites ameliorated with dual modifying mechanisms exhibit much enhanced photodegradation activity of methylene blue than bare one. Additionally, experiment parameters have been optimized and the mechanism of enhancement of photocatalytic activity is also discussed in detail.

All the reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. The SnO2 nanorods were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method. In the typical synthesis, 6mmol sodium stannate (Na2SnO3) was dissolved in 20mL deionized water under magnetic stirring. After stirring for 30min at room temperature, 60mL absolute ethyl alcohol was slowly added, stirring for 60min. Then the above solution was transferred into a dried Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave with a capacity of 100mL at ambient temperature, followed by hydrothermal treatment of the mixture at 210°C for 48h. After hydrothermal reaction, the white precipitate was collected by centrifuge, washed with deionized water and ethylalcohol for three times, and then dried in an oven at 60°C for 24h.

The SnO2-GQDs nanocomposites were synthesized firstly by calcining the mixtures of SnO2 and citric acid. To put it simply, SnO2 powder was mixed with CA by a mass ratio of SnO2 to CA (5/1) in the compound. Then, the mixture was transferred to a crucible and calcinated at 200°C for 3h in the air. After the pyrolytic reaction of CA, the SnO2/GQDs nanocomposites were obtained. Afterwards, the gained powder was calcinated in the nitrogen gas environment in a muffle furnace. A typical reduction reaction between GQDs and SnO2 occurred during the process. To explore the effect of the concentration of VOs generated in the reduction course on the photocatalytic performance of SnO2, SnO2-GQDs nanocomposites was calcinated at different temperature (350°C, 450°C, 550°C, 650°C) in the nitrogen gas environment. The obtained materials were labeled as GQDs@SnO2-VOs-3, GQDs@SnO2-VOs-4, GQDs@SnO2-VOs-5 and GQDs@SnO2-VOs-6, respectively.

Structural properties of all the samples were carried out using X-ray diffraction (XRD) with an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker D8 ADVANCE) equipped with X’Pert High Score under Cu Kα (λ=1.5405Å) radiation. The X-ray machine was operated at 45kV whereas the scanning range of (2θ) was from 10° to 90°. Morphological studies of the synthesized materials were performed by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL JSM-2010). UV–vis absorption spectrum was obtained using a UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu UV-3600). Raman spectra were recorded on a Raman spectrometer (Renishaw InVia). Electron paramagnetic resonance spectra (EPR) were recorded at 110K on a Bruker EMX-10/12 EPR spectrometer. Thermogravimetric analysis (TG) was conducted using a Pyris 1 DSC analyzer (PerkineElmer).

Photodegradation experiments were carried out in a 250mL beaker with 0.1g photocatalysts and solution of MB (7.5ppm, 200mL). Before exposure to UV–vis light irradiation, the suspension was stirred in the dark for 30min, thereby establishing absorption-desorption equilibrium between the organic molecules and the catalyst surface. A 500-W Xe lamp was employed as the light source. The light was positioned 15cm away from the reactor. At each irradiation time interval, 5mL of the suspension was collected and separated through centrifugation. All of the measurements were carried out at room temperature. The spectral response of the centrifuged sample solution was recorded by UV–vis spectrophotometer.

The XRD patterns of the pure SnO2 nanorods, GQDs@SnO2 compounds, and annealed GQDs@SnO2 with various temperatures are shown in Fig. 1 . The patterns revealed the samples GQDs@SnO2 composites and GQDs-SnO2 calcined at 350–650°C (GQDs@SnO2-VOs-3 to GQDs@SnO2-VOs-6) contain all rutile phase of tin oxide (PDF#041-1445). It is perceptible that both pure SnO2 and the prepared samples grow along the preferred orientation of (101), (110), and (211) with considerable intensities. The presence of other orientations such as (200), (220), (002), (310), (301), (112), (202), (321), (222) also have been detected for all the samples. It is suggested that GQDs@SnO2 calcined at different temperatures basically retain the crystal structures of tin oxide. Besides, graphene cannot be detected which could be attributed to the low amount and relatively low diffraction intensity of GQDs.

The existence of GQDs and the effect of preparation condition on GQDs graphitization degree were confirmed by Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 2 ). The sample calcined at 350°C, 450°C, 550°C and 650°C display various intensity of characteristic bands at 1553cm−1 and 1386cm−1, corresponding to the graphite (G band, first-order scattering of E2g phonons by sp2 carbon atoms) and diamondoid (D band, breathing mode of κ-point photons of A1g symmetry) bands, respectively [28–30]. The intensity ratio of the G and D band (IG/ID) of Raman spectroscopy can reflect the degree of graphitization. The supreme IG/ID value of GQDs@SnO2-VOs-4 indicates its best degree of graphitization, which results from the comparatively severe reaction process between GQDs and SnO2 at 450°C.

The morphology and microstructure of the pure SnO2 and GQDs@SnO2-VOs-4 samples were examined by TEM and HRTEM. Parts a, b and c of Fig. 3 exhibit the representative TEM images of SnO2 rods. The crystal lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.334nm can be assigned to the (110) plane of SnO2 (Fig. 3h), which is in accordance with the XRD results as well as the FFT plots show. The rutile phase of tin oxide shows apparently nanorod feature with width and length of about 10nm and 160nm, respectively. Fig. 3d–f is typical TEM images of the products, clearly showing that many small GQDs (approximately 2–3nm) are uniformly dispersed on the surface of SnO2 nanorod. Notably, a few areas with much brighter contrast can be noticed along the SnO2 nanorod from Fig. 3g and h, which may results from the porous structure produced during the redox reaction process following the generation of oxygen vacancies. The porosity of the sample was confirmed by the nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms (Fig. 3i). The isotherm of the GQDs@SnO2-VOs-4 sample exhibits a typical IUPAC type IV pattern with a sharp upturn in high-relative-pressure region. The hysteresis loop of the specimen has a combination of H1 and H3 characters that are corresponding to cylindrical and slit-shaped pore geometries structures, respectively. The BET specific surface area of the product is ca. 42.1m2 g−1 and the pore size distribution curve based on the BJH method (inset) revealed average pore diameters in the range of 2–50nm of mesoporous.

Photoabsorption is a crucial factor affecting the photocatalytic performance. Fig. 4 shows the UV–vis absorbance spectra of pure SnO2 (marked in black), GQDs@SnO2 composites (red) and GQDs@SnO2 calcined at 350°C (blue), 450°C (magenta) and 550°C (olive). The pure SnO2 shows no absorption at λ>400nm caused by wide energy band gap of 3.6eV. Feeble enhancement of photoabsorption is observed for the precursor of GQDs@SnO2 composites, mainly derived from the photoresponse of GQDs to visible-light. Meanwhile, all the prepared GQDs@SnO2-VOs samples exhibit highly enhanced absorption at λ>400nm, covering nearly the entire visible range. With the increase of visible-light response, the as-prepared stuffs behave different degrees of improvements to visible-light absorption. It is worth mentioning that the GQDs@SnO2-VOs samples enjoy huge ascension of photoabsorption compared to other samples, which plays a major role in its best photocatalytic performance. In fact, the improvement of photoresponse primarily originated from the effect of VOs.

The relative quantity of GQDs in the composites after the reduction reactions between tin oxide and GQDs was determined by TG analysis, as shown in Fig. 6 . The thermogravimetry tests were carried out in air with a heating rate of 10°C/min. Obviously, various percentages of GQDs left in the composites and the weight loss is 7.68%, 4.17%, 5.93%, 2.92% from the sample GQDs@SnO2-VOs-3, GQDs@SnO2-VOs-4, GQDs@SnO2-VOs-5 to GQDs@SnO2-VOs-6, corresponding to the surplus percentage content of GQDs in the compounds, respectively. Particularly worth mentioning is that the relative content of GQDs in the sample GQDs@SnO2-VOs-4 is low with weight percent of 4.17%. In view of the down-to-earth conditions and ultima photocatalyic consequence, we infer that GQDs react vigorously with SnO2 at 450°C (in accordance with the result in Fig. 2), resulting in skimp GQDs left. Most often, GQDs can give rise to a certain degree of enhancement for the photocatalytic performance by effective transfer of electrons like noble metal and a certain response to visible light. In our work, GQDs acts a minor character owing to its low content, while the VOs dominate the photocatalytic property. As a result, GQDs@SnO2-VOs-3 with maximum amount of GQDs shows subaltern photocatalytics compared to GQDs@SnO2-VOs-4.

Methylene blue is a brightly colored blue cationic thiazine dye and often used as a test model pollutant in semiconductor photocatalysis. Fig. 7 a shows the photocatalytic activity of pure SnO2, GQDs@SnO2 nanocomposites and GQDs@SnO2-VOs nanocompounds towards MB degradation under UV–vis light irradiation. It can be observed that the as-prepared samples show tremendous enhancements of photocatalytic performance in contrast to pure SnO2. By reason of a certain degree of visible-light responds as well as effective separation of hole-electron pairs, the sample GQDs@SnO2 exhibits preferable degradation behavior than pure SnO2 and GQDs@SnO2-VOs-6. With more GQDs left in the compound, the samples calcined at 350°C possess fine property of degradation next only to GQDs@SnO2-VOs-4, by which MB solution was almost fully degraded and became transparent within ∼90min, for the leading role of VOs. As shown in Fig. 7b, the rate constants (calculated using ln(C/Co) vs. time) of GQDs@SnO2 nanocomposites and GQDs@SnO2-VOs nanocompounds are 0.00778min−1, 0.01175min−1, 0.01668min−1, 0.0104min−1 and 0.00562min−1, respectively, which show diverse degrees of improvements for photodegradation relative to pure SnO2. It is worth nothing that there are ca. 5.8 times enhancement of photocatalytic activity for MB degradation in terms of rate constant for GQDs@SnO2-VOs-4, compared to pure SnO2 (0.00286min−1). To demonstrate the extended photoresponse of SnO2 from UV to visible light region, we carried out degradation test of as-prepared samples under visible-light irradiation (Fig. 7c). SnO2 has no absorption ability for visible light with the narrow band gap of 3.6eV, therefore enhanced visible light response is the primary step for effective degradation. Graphene quantum dots can decrease the recombination of hole and electrons efficiently, but their effect on the response to visible light is poor. With weaker visible-light response and accompanying less facilitating function of charge transfer, the photocatalytic performance of GQDs@SnO2 composites is merely better than pure SnO2. Meanwhile, GQDs@SnO2-VOs compounds show varying degrees of enhancements towards MB degradation compared with SnO2. With the dominated effect of VOs and assistant function of GQDs, GQDs@SnO2-VOs-4 sample exhibits the best visible-light degradation over MB, and the other samples exert alike ranks as the behavior under UV–vis light irradiation. The corresponding rate constants are presented in Fig. 7d, the rate constant for GQDs@SnO2-VOs-4 (0.01056min−1) is 33.6 times better than pure SnO2 (3.1416×10−4 min−1), which basically has no degradation ability.

The photocatalytic mechanism was related with the active species. In order to investigate the main active species which may exist in the photodegradation process, two radical scavengers were added to the system. The effects of the two scavengers on the degradation of MB were researched in 150min under visible light irradiation to illuminate the photocatalytic mechanism. Sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4) and silver nitrate (AgNO3) were used as photogenerated holes (h+), photogenerated electrons (e−) scavenger, respectively. To get more reliable test results, three parallel experiments were conducted, and we use the average as the reference values. As shown in Fig. 8 , without the addition of any scavengers, the degradation efficiency of MB over GQDs@SnO2-VOs-4 sample was 83.19% after 150min of visible light irradiation. The photodegradation efficiency dropped to 46.62% and 64.42% after the addition of Na2C2O4 and AgNO3, respectively. The obvious inhibitory effects of Na2C2O4 and AgNO3 were about 36.53% and 18.77%, indicating that both photogenerated h+ and e− play vital role in the photocatalytic process and h+ contributes more.

On the basis of the above empirical data as well as analysis results, a possible mechanism for enhanced photocatalytic performance of GQDs@SnO2-VOs nanocomposites are presented in Fig. 9 . SnO2 could only absorb the ultraviolet light because of its wide band gap of 3.6eV, the VOs states form below the conduction band in un-doped SnO2, obviously promoting the visible-light absorption and the generation of the photoexcited electron–hole pairs over the surface of SnO2, which can be observed obviously from the degradation (Fig. 7c). Subsequently, the decorated GQDs can accept the photogenerated electrons and prolong the lifetime of charge carriers. Also, GQDs can give rise to response to visible light to some extent. The comparison of the degradation efficiency of GQDs@SnO2-VOs-4 composites for the photodegradation of MB with/without scavengers in Fig. 8 amply demonstrates the important role of photogenerated h+ and e−, thus indicating the significant function of oxygen vacancies and GQDs. Therefore, the efficiency towards photocatalytic redox process is improved. The adsorbed MB in solution is oxidized to carbon dioxide and water by photogenerated holes. Simultaneously, the photogenerated electrons trapped by GQDs can also be captured by oxygen to produce superoxide radicals. These oxygen-containing species (O2 or O2
−) then are able to selectively oxidize the MB, further giving rise to the rate of photocatalysis. Based on the above analysis, the photocatalytic activity of GQDs@SnO2-VOs nanocomposites is tremendously enhanced with the dominated effect of VOs and assistant function of GQDs.

To summarize, an accessible strategy has been put forward to fabricate defect-modulation SnO2 with GQDs, combining the advantage of oxygen vacancy doping and noble metal deposition. The extensional optical absorption induced by VOs and effective separation of electron-hole pairs resulted from GQDs synthetically contribute to the prominent photodegradation activity of GQDs@SnO2-VOs composites. On account of relative small quantity of GQDs left in the GQDs@SnO2-VOs composites, VOs acts as a prominent role in the photocatalysis. Diverse reaction temperatures have been attempted and GQDs@SnO2-VOs-4 nano-compounds reveal the optimal photocatalytic performance as a consequence of the relatively high concentrations of VOs of all the prepared samples. Meanwhile, a possible mechanism for enhanced photocatalytic performance has also been proposed on the basis of the obtained experimental data as well as analysis results. With the realization of visible-light response as well as raised charge-separation efficiency simultaneously by a simple strategy, more materials system are expected to be applied in this way and it should be interesting and helpful for readers in the related field.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the National Nature Science Foundation of China (No. 51172109), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. NS2014057), the Open Research Fund of Jiangsu Provincial Key Laboratory for Nanotechnology of Nanjing University, the Qing Lan Project, and the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).
